content.problemStatement:
Chef has a sequence of positive integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. He wants to choose some elements of this sequence (possibly none or all of them) and compute their MEX, i.e. the smallest positive integer which does not occur among the chosen elements. For example, the MEX of [1,2,4][1, 2, 4][1,2,4] is 333.

Help Chef find the largest number of elements of the sequence AAA which he can choose such that their MEX is equal to MMM, or determine that it is impossible.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and M. The second line contains N space‑separated positive integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible size of a chosen subset whose MEX equals M, or -1 if no such subset exists.

sampleTestCases.explanation: All numbers 1 and 2 are present, 3 is absent. We can take the whole array (size 3). Its MEX is 3.
For MEX = 2 we need number 1 in the chosen subset, but 1 does not appear in the array. Hence impossible.
MEX = 1 requires no number <1. We must avoid all 1's. There are two 1's, so we can pick the remaining 4 elements.

hidden.label: general case possible with some M's

solution.approach:
For a subset to have MEX = M it must contain every integer from 1 to M‑1 at least once and must not contain M. Any element larger than M can be taken freely. Therefore:
1. Count how many times each value appears (we only need counts for numbers ≤ M).
2. If any i in [1, M‑1] is absent, the answer is -1.
3. Otherwise we can take all elements except those equal to M. The maximal size is N – count[M].
The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case using a hash map (or an array of size M+1) for counting.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(M) for the counting map (at most O(N) in the worst case).
